titleStmt.title: Letter from Hugh O'Brien Moran to Augustine Birrell, 4 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Hugh O'Brien Moran

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from a Limerick based solicitor Hugh O'Brien Moran to Chief Secretary of Ireland Augustine Birrell (1850-1933). Moran is writing to Birrell to complain about the treatment of his clients who are currently imprisoned in Limerick Prison. Moran claims that he is being refused to visit his clients on a professional level by the authorities and this is against their rights. He also claims that they are being held under the Protection of the Realm Act, when actually they were arrested before the declaration of martial lawAugustine Birrell served as the chief secretary of Ireland from 1907 to 1916. Birrell was a liberal politician praised for his reforms of the Irish education system. The Easter Rising caught him unawares and, occupied in London, there was little he could do. A later inquiry into the Rising held Birrell chiefly responsible for the events that occurred leading to his resignation on 1 May 1916.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/7571/1

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Hugh O'Brien Moran
Solicitor
Branch Office,
Killaloe, Co.Clare. 19, Glentworth Street, Limerick 4th May 1916 Right Hon Sir. I beg, most respectfully, to draw your attention
to the following matters. I have been instructed to
represent the following person, in my professional capacity,
namely:â
Samuel Windrim, of Limerick.
Patrick Fahy, of Gort, Co Galway,
John Coen, of Ardrahan, Co Galway.
James Wall, of Tralee, Co Kerry.
James Quigley, of Limerick, and
Denis Healy, of Bodyke, Co Clare.
All the above named persons are at present confined in the
Prison at Limerick, and are charged with Offences alleged
to have been commmitted under the Defence of the Realm Act
, and most of them, were actually arrested before the Proclamâ
ation of Martial Law. Whatn I desire to respectfully draw
your attention to, is, that the Military Authority here at
Limerick, Golonel Colonel, Sir. A. Welson, has refused me
permission to interview, my Clients, at the Prison, whenever I
require to see and consult them professionally. Colonel
Weldon informed me that when these prisoners are about to be
Coutmarialled (if they ever are), I will receive Notice of
Hugh O'Brien Moran,
Solicitor.
Branch Office,
Killaloe, Co. Clare.
19, Glentworth Street, Limerick . No 2.
Hugh O'Brien Moran.
Solicitor.
Branch Office,
Killaloe, Co. Clare.
19, Glentworth Street, Limerick. No. 3
